sections.title: Overview
sections.text:
DRAFT PROFILE: BIO
Eric McAlister grew up in Azle, Texas, about 20 miles northwest of Fort Worth, and established himself as one of the state's premier high school receivers. During his senior year at Azle High, he hauled in 70 catches for 1,498 yards and 20 touchdowns while leading his team to back-to-back district titles. Those numbers earned him three-star status across the major recruiting services and recognition as one of the top 100 receivers nationally according to 247Sports. He signed with Boise State and redshirted in 2021 before beginning his collegiate career in earnest.

McAlister's time in Boise showed steady growth. After limited action as a redshirt freshman in 2022 where he averaged an eye-popping 23.55 yards per catch on 11 receptions and four scores, he broke out as a sophomore with 47 catches for 873 yards and five touchdowns across nine games. The production earned attention, but McAlister wanted to come home. He transferred to TCU ahead of the 2024 season, joining a loaded receiver room that included Savion Williams, Jack Bech, and JP Richardson. Despite the competition for targets, he carved out a significant role with 39 receptions for 762 yards and five touchdowns while leading the nation in first-down catch percentage at 89.7 percent on receptions.

Everything clicked during his senior year in Fort Worth. With Williams and Bech gone to the NFL, McAlister became quarterback Josh Hoover's clear number one option and responded with one of the best receiving seasons in TCU history. He finished with 1,190 receiving yards, second-most ever by a Horned Frog wideout, and 10 touchdowns. His 254-yard, three-touchdown performance against SMU stands as the second-best single-game receiving total in program history. The season earned him third-team All-American honors from the Associated Press, first-team All-Big 12 recognition, and a spot as a Biletnikoff Award semifinalist. He capped his career by choosing to play in the Alamo Bowl despite losing his starting quarterback to the transfer portal, catching eight passes for 69 yards in TCU's win over USC.

Watch that SMU game again and you'll see a receiver who can take over when the moment demands it. Three touchdowns, 254 yards, multiple broken tackles turning short catches into explosive plays. McAlister possesses the size, speed, and competitive fire that translates to Sunday football. His ability to win contested catches and create after the catch provides immediate value, even if his route-running needs continued refinement at the next level.



Here's the deal with McAlister: he profiles as a complementary receiver who can develop into a legitimate number two option with the right coaching. His best fit comes in offenses that emphasize vertical concepts and give receivers opportunities to win one-on-one matchups down the field. Zone-heavy schemes that ask receivers to find soft spots and settle will also work, as his awareness of coverage holes showed up repeatedly in his college tape. Teams running West Coast principles that demand precise timing and sharp breaks on every route may find his inconsistent stem work frustrating.



The intangibles matter here. McAlister overcame off-field trouble early in his TCU career and responded by becoming one of the most respected leaders in that locker room. When his starting quarterback entered the transfer portal before a bowl game, McAlister stood in front of his teammates and rallied support for the backup. He then went out and performed when it counted. That type of character, combined with production that speaks for itself, makes him a worthwhile investment for teams looking to add depth and competition to their receiver room.
